text: A Norwegian breast cancer screening program analyzed mortality outcomes to assess the impact of screening on breast cancer deaths. Initiated in 1996 and gradually expanded over nine years, the program invited women meeting specific criteria to undergo biennial screening, creating four comparison groups: counties with screening versus without, plus two historical cohorts from a decade earlier. Analyzing data from 40,075 women, the study observed a mortality reduction of about one-third among those offered screening, corresponding to roughly 2.4 fewer deaths per 100,000. The findings suggest that offering breast cancer screening can reduce breast cancer mortality.

A Norwegian study found that offering biennial mammography screening to women aged 50–90 reduced breast cancer deaths, though modestly. Comparing regions with and without screening and against historical data showed a total reduction of 7.2 deaths per year in areas with screening, of which 2.4 deaths per year were attributable to the screening program alone. Although the impact is small, it is meaningful and should not be discounted.

Norway initiated its breast cancer screening program in 1996 to address the challenge of assessing the effectiveness of mammograms in reducing mortality rates among comparable populations. Previous data proved inadequate as it failed to consider advancements in breast cancer treatment and awareness at the time. The program provided biennial mammograms to women aged 50 to 69. Results were compared with similar cohorts of women from countries that offered screening, those from countries without screening, and two historical groups with comparable screening experiences. The analysis included data from over 40,000 women diagnosed with breast cancer. Among those who underwent screenings, there was a 7.2 deaths per 100,000 reduction in mortality rates, translating to a 10% decrease in death rates. This evidence supports the conclusion that screening effectively lowers the risk of mortality.

While mammography is the preferred method for early detection of breast cancer in women, a long-term study reveals significant findings. Effective screening programs have led to a notable rise in early-stage cancer cases and a decline in late-stage cases. Post-mammography introduction, early-stage cases doubled in a year, while late-stage cases decreased. The study suggests overdiagnosis in 1.3 million U.S. women over 30 years, indicating that screening marginally reduces advanced cancer diagnoses. Overdiagnosis accounts for nearly a third of new breast cancer cases, and screening has a minimal impact on the mortality rate from the disease.

Thirty years of research indicates that breast cancer screening programs have not reduced the progression to advanced disease. For screening to be effective, it must both detect cancers early and lower the later incidence of cancer. This study analyzes three cancer-detection data sets from 1976–2008. Early results were encouraging: US screening mammography doubled the annual detection of early-stage cases, and late-stage cancers declined by about 8%. Nevertheless, overdiagnosis is substantial, with estimates suggesting at least 1.3 million overdiagnosed cases in the last three decades; in 2008, roughly 31% of breast cancer diagnoses were deemed overdiagnosed. These findings imply that mammography screening may have only a modest impact on reducing fatal breast cancers.

This abstract summarizes evidence that adjuvant aromatase inhibitors improve disease-free and breast cancer–free survival in hormone-receptor positive breast cancer compared with tamoxifen, in both postmenopausal and premenopausal women. In postmenopausal patients, aromatase inhibitors show superior outcomes over tamoxifen, with comparable overall survival and safety profiles. In premenopausal women, exemestane plus ovarian suppression yields higher 5-year disease-free and breast cancer freedom relative to tamoxifen plus ovarian suppression in two phase 3 trials involving about 4,700 participants, while overall survival and grade 3/4 adverse events do not differ significantly between groups. These findings align with prior studies supporting the benefit of adjuvant aromatase inhibitors over tamoxifen in this setting.
